title: Good leaf node pairs within distance D
description: Count the number of pairs of leaf nodes whose shortest path distance is less than or equal to a given value D

examples.explanation: Step 1: Collect leaves via DFS. Tree: root=1, left=2, right=3, node2 has right=4. DFS: Node1 (not leaf), go left to node2 (not leaf), go right to node4 (leaf, no children), add 4. Back to node2, done. Back to node1, go right to node3 (leaf, no children), add 3. Leaves = [4,3]. Step 2: Check pairs. Pair (4,3): Find LCA. From root1: path to 4: 1→2→4, path to 3: 1→3. LCA=1. Distances: dist(4)=2, dist(3)=1, dist(LCA)=0. Distance=2+1-2×0=3. D=3, so distance ≤ D. Count=1. Return 1.

Step 1: Collect leaves. Tree is full binary tree with nodes 1-7. DFS: Node1 (not leaf), left to 2 (not leaf), left to 4 (leaf), add 4. Back to 2, right to 5 (leaf), add 5. Back to 1, right to 3 (not leaf), left to 6 (leaf), add 6, right to 7 (leaf), add 7. Leaves=[4,5,6,7]. Step 2: Check all 6 pairs. Pair (4,5): LCA=2, dist(4)=2, dist(5)=2, dist(LCA)=1, distance=2+2-2=2≤4, count=1. (4,6): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=2. (4,7): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=3. (5,6): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=4. (5,7): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=5. (6,7): LCA=3, dist=2+2-2=2≤4, count=6. Return 6.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) with DFS
approaches.summary: Perform DFS to collect all leaf nodes, then compute pairwise distances by traversing the tree to find LCA for each pair. Use it for small trees or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Good Leaf Pairs with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count leaf node pairs within distance D using DFS to collect leaves and pairwise LCA distance computation.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: first identify all leaf nodes in the tree, then check every possible pair of leaves to see if their distance is ≤ D. The distance between two nodes in a tree can be computed as distance(u) + distance(v) - 2×distance(LCA(u,v)), where LCA is the lowest common ancestor. We use DFS to collect all leaf nodes, then for each pair (u,v), we perform another DFS to find their LCA and compute the distance. This approach works because in a tree, the shortest path between any two nodes is unique and passes through their LCA. The algorithm: 1. Perform DFS to collect all leaf nodes in a list. 2. For each pair (i,j) where i<j: a) Find LCA of leaves[i] and leaves[j] using DFS. b) Compute distance from root to each node and LCA. c) Calculate distance = dist(u) + dist(v) - 2×dist(LCA). d) If distance ≤ D, increment count. 3. Return count. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have O(L²) pairs where L is number of leaves (worst case L≈n), and each LCA computation takes O(n). Space is O(n) for recursion stack and storing leaves. This connects to L1 by using the LCA finding subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check i<j to avoid duplicate pairs, incorrect distance formula, or not handling empty/single-node trees. Real code pattern: def countPairs(root, D): leaves=[]; dfs_collect(root, leaves); count=0; for i in range(len(leaves)): for j in range(i+1,len(leaves)): lca=findLCA(root,leaves[i],leaves[j]); dist=distances[leaves[i]]+distances[leaves[j]]-2*distances[lca]; if dist<=D: count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Collect leaves via DFS. Tree: root=1, left=2, right=3, node2 has right=4. DFS: Node1 (not leaf), go left to node2 (not leaf), go right to node4 (leaf, no children), add 4. Back to node2, done. Back to node1, go right to node3 (leaf, no children), add 3. Leaves = [4,3]. Step 2: Check pairs. Pair (4,3): Find LCA. From root1: path to 4: 1→2→4, path to 3: 1→3. LCA=1. Distances: dist(4)=2, dist(3)=1, dist(LCA)=0. Distance=2+1-2×0=3. D=3, so distance ≤ D. Count=1. Return 1.

Step 1: Collect leaves. Tree is full binary tree with nodes 1-7. DFS: Node1 (not leaf), left to 2 (not leaf), left to 4 (leaf), add 4. Back to 2, right to 5 (leaf), add 5. Back to 1, right to 3 (not leaf), left to 6 (leaf), add 6, right to 7 (leaf), add 7. Leaves=[4,5,6,7]. Step 2: Check all 6 pairs. Pair (4,5): LCA=2, dist(4)=2, dist(5)=2, dist(LCA)=1, distance=2+2-2=2≤4, count=1. (4,6): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=2. (4,7): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=3. (5,6): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=4. (5,7): LCA=1, dist=2+2-0=4≤4, count=5. (6,7): LCA=3, dist=2+2-2=2≤4, count=6. Return 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) via DFS
ladders.desc: Find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a binary tree using depth-first search.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial for computing distances between leaf pairs. The intuition: the LCA of two nodes is the deepest node that is an ancestor to both. We use DFS to traverse the tree recursively. For each node, we check if it matches either target node. The algorithm: 1. Perform DFS from root. 2. If current node is null, return null. 3. If current node equals p or q, return current node. 4. Recursively search left and right subtrees. 5. If both left and right return non-null, current node is LCA. 6. Otherwise, return whichever side is non-null (or null if both null). This works because the first node where both targets appear in different subtrees (or the node itself is a target) is the LCA. Time complexity is O(n) per LCA query as we may traverse the entire tree. Space is O(h) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the LCA needed for distance computation. Common mistakes: not handling cases where one node is ancestor of the other, or assuming binary search tree properties. Real code pattern: def findLCA(root, p, q): if not root or root==p or root==q: return root; left=findLCA(root.left,p,q); right=findLCA(root.right,p,q); if left and right: return root; return left if left else right.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root=1. Not null, not p or q. Step 2: Search left subtree: node=2. Not null, not p or q. Search its left: null, return null. Search its right: node=4. This is p, return 4. So left of node2 returns 4. Right of node2: null, return null. Node2 gets left=4, right=null, so returns 4. Back to root1: left=4. Step 3: Search right subtree of root1: node=3. This is q, return 3. Root1 has left=4, right=3. Both non-null, so root1 is LCA. Return 1.

Step 1: Root=1. Not p or q. Search left: node=2. Not p or q. Search its left: node=4, this is p, return 4. Search its right: node=5, this is q, return 5. Node2 has left=4, right=5, both non-null, so node2 is LCA. Return 2 to root1. Root1 gets left=2, right=... (search right subtree: node=3, not p or q, left null, right null, returns null). Root1 has left=2, right=null, so returns 2. Final LCA=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Distance Between Two Nodes Using LCA
ladders.desc: Calculate the shortest path distance between any two nodes in a tree given their LCA.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for determining if leaf pairs are within distance D. The intuition: in a tree, the unique path between two nodes u and v goes up from u to their LCA, then down to v. The distance can be computed as dist(u) + dist(v) - 2×dist(LCA), where dist(x) is the distance from root to x. This works because dist(u) includes the path from root to LCA, and similarly for dist(v), so subtracting twice the LCA distance removes the overlap. Step-by-step: 1. Compute distance from root to each node (e.g., via DFS storing depths). 2. Find LCA of u and v. 3. Distance = depth[u] + depth[v] - 2×depth[LCA]. Time O(1) if depths precomputed (O(n) precomputation), space O(n) for depth storage. This feeds into L1 by providing the distance once LCA is found. Common mistakes: using wrong formula, not ensuring depths are consistent, or misunderstanding that distance is edge count (not node count). Real code pattern: def computeDistance(u, v, depth, lca): return depth[u] + depth[v] - 2*depth[lca].

examples.trace: Given depths: node1 depth=0, node2=1, node3=1, node4=2. u=4 depth=2, v=3 depth=1, LCA=1 depth=0. Compute: 2 + 1 - 2×0 = 3. This matches path: 4→2→1→3 (edges: 4-2,2-1,1-3 = 3 edges).
Depths: node1=0, node2=1, node4=2, node5=2. u=4 depth=2, v=5 depth=2, LCA=2 depth=1. Compute: 2 + 2 - 2×1 = 2. Path: 4→2→5 (edges: 4-2,2-5 = 2 edges).

ladders.title: Building Block — Collect All Leaf Nodes via DFS
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree using DFS to identify and store all leaf nodes.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed to get the list of leaves for pairwise checking. The intuition: a leaf node has no children (both left and right are null). We perform DFS (pre-order, in-order, or post-order) and whenever we encounter a leaf, we add it to a list. Step-by-step: 1. Start at root. 2. If node is null, return. 3. If node has no left and no right child, it's a leaf: add to list. 4. Recursively process left subtree. 5. Recursively process right subtree. This works because DFS visits every node exactly once. Time O(n) where n is number of nodes. Space O(h) for recursion stack, plus O(L) for storing leaves (L ≤ n). This feeds into L2 by providing the leaves for distance computation. Common mistakes: incorrectly identifying leaves (e.g., nodes with one null child are not leaves), or modifying the tree during traversal. Real code pattern: def collectLeaves(root, leaves): if not root: return; if not root.left and not root.right: leaves.append(root); collectLeaves(root.left, leaves); collectLeaves(root.right, leaves).

examples.trace: Step 1: Root=1, has left and right, not leaf. Step 2: Go left to node2, has right child, not leaf. Step 3: Go right from node2 to node4, no children, leaf → add 4. Step 4: Back to node2, done. Back to root1, go right to node3, no children, leaf → add 3. Leaves collected: [4,3].

Step 1: Root=1, not leaf. Step 2: Left to node2, not leaf. Left to node4, no children, leaf → add 4. Back to node2, right to node5, leaf → add 5. Back to root1, right to node3, leaf → add 3. Leaves: [4,5,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) Traversal of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse all nodes in a binary tree using depth-first search (pre-order example).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used to explore the tree structure. The intuition: DFS goes as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. For binary trees, common orders are pre-order (root, left, right), in-order (left, root, right), or post-order (left, right, root). We'll use pre-order for simplicity. Algorithm: 1. Visit the current node (e.g., print or process it). 2. Recursively traverse left subtree. 3. Recursively traverse right subtree. Base case: if node is null, return. This works because recursion naturally implements the depth-first strategy. Time O(n) visits every node once. Space O(h) for recursion stack where h is tree height. This feeds into L3 by providing the traversal mechanism to find leaves. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if base case missing, or confusing traversal orders. Real code pattern: def dfs(node): if not node: return; print(node.val); dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root=1, visit 1. Step 2: Go left to node=2, visit 2. Step 3: Node2 has no left, so return, then no right, return. Back to root1. Step 4: Go right to node=3, visit 3. Step 5: Node3 has no left or right, return. Traversal order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Root=1, visit 1. Step 2: Left to node2, visit 2. Step 3: Node2 left is null, skip. Right to node4, visit 4. Step 4: Node4 has no children, return to node2, then to root1. Step 5: Right to node3, visit 3. Order: 1,2,4,3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Leaf Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree node is and how to identify leaf nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. In code, a node is typically represented as an object with attributes: val (value), left (pointer to left child), right (pointer to right child). A leaf node is a node with no children—both left and right are null. The intuition: leaves are the endpoints of the tree, where traversal stops. To check if a node is a leaf: if node.left is null and node.right is null, then it's a leaf. This is fundamental because our problem focuses on leaf nodes. Time O(1) to check a node, space O(1). This feeds into L4 by defining what we look for during DFS traversal. Common mistakes: confusing leaves with nodes that have one null child (those are not leaves), or misunderstanding null as a valid node. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val=val; self.left=left; self.right=right. def isLeaf(node): return node and node.left is None and node.right is None.

examples.trace: Given node with value 1, left pointer is null, right pointer is null. Check: left is null? Yes. Right is null? Yes. Both conditions true, so it is a leaf node.

Node has value 2, left points to another node (value 3), right is null. Check: left is null? No (it points to a node). Since left is not null, the condition 'left is null and right is null' is false. Therefore, it is not a leaf.

approaches.name: Postorder DP with Distance Arrays O(n * D²)
approaches.summary: Use postorder traversal to compute, for each node, an array of leaf distances in its subtree, then combine arrays from child nodes to count valid pairs within distance D. Suitable for moderate D values where D is not too large.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Good Leaf Pairs with Postorder DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting leaf node pairs within distance D using postorder traversal with distance arrays.
ladders.explanation: This solution uses a bottom-up postorder traversal to compute, for each node, an array `dist[node]` where `dist[node][i]` stores the number of leaf nodes in the subtree rooted at `node` that are exactly distance `i` from `node`. The key insight is that when processing a node, we can combine distance arrays from its children to count valid leaf pairs where the path goes through this node. For each pair of child subtrees, we count leaf pairs where the distance between leaves equals the distance through their parent plus 2 (for the edges from leaves to parent). Specifically, for distances `i` from left child and `j` from right child, the leaf-to-leaf distance is `i + j + 2`. We count pairs where this sum ≤ D. The algorithm recursively computes distance arrays for children, combines them to count pairs, then merges them into the parent's distance array by incrementing distances by 1 (for the edge from child to parent). Time complexity is O(n * D²) because for each node with multiple children, we compare all distance pairs up to D. Space is O(n * D) for storing distance arrays. This approach works well when D is moderate since D² dominates. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle nodes with single children correctly, off-by-one errors in distance calculations, and not pruning distance arrays beyond D to save space. The solution elegantly breaks the problem into subtree computations that combine at parent nodes.

examples.trace: Tree: root=1, left=2 (children 4,5), right=3 (children 6,7). All nodes are leaves except 1,2,3. Process leaf nodes 4,5,6,7: each has dist array [1] at index 0 (1 leaf at distance 0). Node 2: left child 4 gives dist [1], right child 5 gives dist [1]. Combine: for i=0,j=0, distance=0+0+2=2 ≤4, count pairs = 1*1=1. Merge: dist[2] = [0,2] (2 leaves at distance 1 from node 2). Node 3: similar to node 2, dist[3]=[0,2]. Node 1: left child 2 gives dist [0,2], right child 3 gives dist [0,2]. Combine: for i=1,j=1 (distances from leaves to their parents), distance=1+1+2=4 ≤4, count pairs = 2*2=4. Total pairs = 1 (from node 2) + 1 (from node 3) + 4 = 6? Wait, we overcount: leaf pairs are (4,5), (6,7), (4,6), (4,7), (5,6), (5,7). Check distances: (4,5)=2, (6,7)=2, (4,6)=4, (4,7)=4, (5,6)=4, (5,7)=4. All ≤4, so 6 pairs. But our calculation gave 1+1+4=6, correct. Output is 6.

Tree: root=1, left=2, right=null, left child of 2: 3 and 4. Leaves: 3 and 4. Process leaf 3: dist=[1]. Leaf 4: dist=[1]. Node 2: children 3 and 4, combine: i=0,j=0, distance=2 ≤3, count=1. Merge: dist[2]=[0,2]. Node 1: left child 2 gives dist=[0,2], right child null. No combination since only one child. Merge: dist[1]=[0,0,2] (since distance from leaf to root is 2, so at index 2). Total pairs counted at node 2: 1. Output 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Combine Distance Arrays from Child Subtrees
ladders.desc: Count leaf pairs where path goes through current node by combining child distance arrays.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution. Given a node with two children, each child provides a distance array `dist_left` and `dist_right`, where `dist_left[i]` is the number of leaves in the left subtree at exact distance `i` from the left child. To count leaf pairs where the shortest path passes through the current node, we consider leaves from the left and right subtrees. The distance between a leaf in left subtree and a leaf in right subtree is: distance from leaf to left child (i) + distance from leaf to right child (j) + 2 (for edges from left child to node and node to right child). We need to count all pairs (i,j) such that i+j+2 ≤ D. The count is sum over i,j of `dist_left[i] * dist_right[j]` for valid (i,j). Implementation: iterate i from 0 to D-2, j from 0 to D-2-i, accumulate product. Time O(D²) per node with two children. Space O(1) extra. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by being called at each internal node during postorder traversal. Common mistakes: not limiting i and j to D-2, forgetting the +2, or incorrectly handling cases where a child has no leaves (array all zeros). The intuition is that we bridge subtrees at the parent node.

examples.trace: dist_left: at distance 0:0 leaves, 1:3 leaves, 2:1 leaf. dist_right: 0:1 leaf, 1:2 leaves, 2:0. D=4. Valid pairs: i+j+2 ≤4. Iterate i=0 to 2, j=0 to 2. i=0: j=0: 0+0+2=2 ≤4, count=0*1=0; j=1: 0+1+2=3 ≤4, count=0*2=0; j=2: 0+2+2=4 ≤4, count=0*0=0. i=1: j=0: 1+0+2=3 ≤4, count=3*1=3; j=1: 1+1+2=4 ≤4, count=3*2=6; j=2: 1+2+2=5 >4, skip. i=2: j=0: 2+0+2=4 ≤4, count=1*1=1; j=1: 2+1+2=5 >4, skip; j=2: skip. Total = 0+0+0+3+6+0+1+0+0 = 10? Wait, recalc: i=1,j=0:3, i=1,j=1:6, i=2,j=0:1, sum=10. But output should be 8? Check: dist_left[1]=3, dist_right[0]=1 gives 3 pairs, dist_left[1]=3, dist_right[1]=2 gives 6 pairs, dist_left[2]=1, dist_right[0]=1 gives 1 pair, total 10. But D=4, i=2,j=0 gives 2+0+2=4 valid, so 1 pair included. So 10 pairs. Perhaps input meant D=3? Let's assume D=3: then i=1,j=0:3, i=1,j=1: 1+1+2=4 >3 skip, i=2,j=0: 2+0+2=4 >3 skip. So output 3. For this example, use D=3, output 3.

dist_left: 1 leaf at distance 0. dist_right: 2 leaves at distance 1. D=3. i=0,j=1: 0+1+2=3 ≤3, count=1*2=2. So output should be 2. But desc says output 0? Let's correct: output 2. Trace: only pair i=0,j=1 valid, count 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Postorder Traversal with Distance Arrays
ladders.desc: Compute for each node an array of leaf counts at each distance in its subtree.
ladders.explanation: This core technique uses a postorder traversal (left, right, root) to build, for each node, a distance array `dist` where `dist[i]` is the number of leaf nodes in the subtree rooted at this node that are exactly distance `i` from this node. For a leaf node, `dist[0] = 1` (the leaf itself at distance 0). For an internal node, we first recursively get distance arrays from children. Then, to form the node's own array, we merge child arrays: for each child, we shift its array by 1 (since distance from child to parent is 1 edge) and sum element-wise. For example, if left child has array `[a,b]` (meaning a leaves at distance 0, b at distance 1 from left child), then from the parent's perspective, these leaves are at distances 1 and 2. So parent array gets `[0, a, b]` from left child. Similarly for right child, then sum. This allows us to propagate leaf distance information up the tree. The array is truncated at length D+1 since distances beyond D are irrelevant for counting pairs within D. Time O(n * D) for building arrays, as each node processes arrays of size up to D. Space O(n * D) for storing all arrays. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the distance arrays to combine. Intuition: bottom-up aggregation lets each node summarize its subtree compactly. Common mistakes: not initializing arrays correctly for leaves, forgetting to shift by 1, or not handling null children.

examples.input: Node with left child leaf (dist=[1]) and right child leaf (dist=[1])
examples.trace: Left child array: [1] (1 leaf at distance 0 from left child). Right child similar. Merge: shift each by 1: left becomes [0,1], right becomes [0,1]. Sum element-wise: index 0:0+0=0, index 1:1+1=2. So dist=[0,2] for parent. This means from parent, there are 0 leaves at distance 0, 2 leaves at distance 1.

examples.input: Node with left child dist=[0,2] and right child null
examples.trace: Left child array: [0,2] (0 leaves at distance 0, 2 leaves at distance 1 from left child). Right child is null, treated as empty array []. Shift left by 1: becomes [0,0,2]. Sum with right (empty): [0,0,2]. So parent dist indicates 2 leaves at distance 2 from parent.

testCases.input: Node with left child [0,1] and right child [1]
Node with left child [0,0,1] and right child [0,2]
Node with left child [] and right child []
Node with left child [0,3] and right child [0,0,1]
Node with left child [1] and right child [0,1]
Node with left child [0,0,0,1] and right child [0,0,0,1]
Node with left child [2] and right child [1]

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal and Recursion
ladders.desc: Traverse binary tree in postorder using recursion to process children before parent.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a binary tree in postorder: for each node, recursively process the left subtree, then the right subtree, then the node itself. This order is essential because we need information from children (like distance arrays) before we can compute results for the parent. In recursion, we define a function `dfs(node)` that returns a distance array for the subtree rooted at `node`. Base case: if node is null, return empty array; if node is a leaf (both children null), return `[1]`. Recursive case: call `dfs(left)` and `dfs(right)`, get arrays, then combine as needed. Time O(n) for visiting all nodes, space O(h) for recursion stack where h is tree height. This feeds into core logic by providing the framework to compute distance arrays bottom-up. Intuition: postorder ensures dependencies are satisfied. Common mistakes: not handling null nodes correctly, infinite recursion if base cases missed, or using preorder/inorder by accident. Real code pattern: define helper function that returns array.

examples.trace: Start at root 1. Call dfs(1): first call dfs(left=2). dfs(2): left child null, right child null, so leaf, return [1]. Back to dfs(1): call dfs(right=3). dfs(3): leaf, return [1]. Now process node 1: has arrays from children, combine. So traversal processes 2, then 3, then 1.
dfs(1): call dfs(2). dfs(2): call dfs(left=4). dfs(4): leaf, return [1]. dfs(2): right null, so process node 2 with left array [1], return shifted array. Then dfs(1): process node 1. Order: 4,2,1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Manipulation: Shift and Sum
ladders.desc: Shift an array by prepending zero and sum two arrays element-wise.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key array manipulations: 1) Shift an array by prepending a zero to represent increasing distances by 1. For example, shifting `[a,b,c]` gives `[0,a,b,c]`. This corresponds to moving from child to parent: distances from leaves to child become distances to parent plus 1. 2) Sum two arrays element-wise: if arrays are `[a1,a2,...]` and `[b1,b2,...]`, result is `[a1+b1, a2+b2, ...]`, padding with zeros if lengths differ. This combines leaf counts from multiple children. Time O(max(len1, len2)) per operation. Space O(max(len1, len2)) for result. This feeds into building block by being used in merging child distance arrays. Intuition: shifting accounts for extra edge, summing aggregates counts. Common mistakes: off-by-one in shifting, not handling empty arrays, or incorrect padding during sum.

examples.trace: Start with array [1,2,3]. Prepend 0: new array [0,1,2,3]. This means if original had counts at distances 0,1,2, after shift they are at distances 1,2,3.
Array1: [1,0,2]. Array2: [0,3,1]. Element-wise sum: index 0: 1+0=1, index 1: 0+3=3, index 2: 2+1=3. Result [1,3,3].

testCases.input: Shift and sum: shift [1] then sum with [0,1]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree and Leaf Nodes
ladders.desc: Identify leaf nodes in a binary tree and understand tree structure.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. A leaf node is a node with no children (both left and right are null). In this problem, we focus on pairs of leaf nodes. To work with trees, we need to traverse nodes and check for leaf condition. For example, given a tree represented as a list in level order (like [1,2,3] where 1 is root, 2 is left child, 3 is right child), we can build a tree structure. In code, a TreeNode class typically has attributes val, left, right. A leaf is identified by `if node.left is None and node.right is None`. This foundation is prerequisite because the problem counts pairs among leaves. Understanding tree traversal (like DFS) builds on this. Time O(1) to check if a node is leaf, space O(1). Common mistakes: confusing leaf with internal node, or misinterpreting tree representation.

examples.trace: Node 1 has children 2 and 3, so not leaf. Node 2 has no children (assuming null), so leaf. Node 3 has no children, so leaf. Leaves are 2 and 3.
Node 1 has left child 2, right null, so not leaf. Node 2 has left child 4, right null, so not leaf. Node 4 has no children, so leaf. Only leaf is 4.

approaches.name: Centroid Decomposition O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Apply centroid decomposition to recursively break the tree into subtrees, count leaf pairs across different subtrees using distance tracking, and sum results. Optimal for large trees with arbitrary D, as it reduces the problem size logarithmically.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Good Leaf Pairs Using Centroid Decomposition
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting leaf node pairs with distance ≤ D using centroid decomposition.
ladders.explanation: This solution uses centroid decomposition to efficiently count leaf pairs with distance ≤ D in O(n log n) time. The intuition is to recursively decompose the tree by finding centroids (nodes whose removal splits the tree into subtrees of size ≤ n/2). At each centroid, we count leaf pairs where the path passes through that centroid. We do this by collecting distances from the centroid to all leaves in its subtree, then counting pairs from different child subtrees whose sum of distances ≤ D. This avoids double-counting by ensuring pairs are only counted at their lowest common ancestor in the decomposition tree. The algorithm: 1) Find centroid of current subtree. 2) For each child subtree of centroid, collect distances from centroid to all leaves in that subtree. 3) For each pair of child subtrees, count leaf pairs where distance1 + distance2 ≤ D using a two-pointer or frequency array approach. 4) Recursively process each child subtree after removing centroid. Time: O(n log n) due to log n decomposition levels, each processing O(n) total nodes. Space: O(n) for recursion and distance arrays. Connection to L1: Uses the centroid finding and pair counting subroutines. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark centroids as removed, double-counting pairs, inefficient pair counting. Real code pattern: def count_pairs(root, D): total = 0; centroid = find_centroid(root); for child in centroid.children: distances = collect_distances(child, centroid); total += count_pairs_between(distances, D); for child in centroid.children: total += count_pairs(child, D); return total.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find centroid of full tree (node 1). Step 2: Collect distances from centroid 1: Child subtree 2: distances [2,2] (leaves 4,5 at distance 2); Child subtree 3: distances [2,2] (leaves 6,7). Step 3: Count pairs between subtrees: For distances [2,2] and [2,2], pairs: (2+2=4≤4): 2*2=4 pairs. Step 4: Recursively process subtrees after removing 1: Subtree rooted at 2 (nodes 2,4,5): centroid=2, no child subtrees with leaves, count=0. Subtree rooted at 3: similarly count=0. Step 5: Total = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 pairs? Wait, we also need pairs within same subtree counted recursively. Actually, pairs within subtree 2: leaves 4,5 distance 2≤4: 1 pair. Similarly subtree 3: 1 pair. So total = 4 (across) + 1 + 1 = 6 pairs.

Step 1: Centroid of full tree (node 1 or 2? Let's compute: Total nodes=5. From 1: max subtree size=3 (nodes 2,4,5). From 2: max subtree size=3 (nodes 1,3). From 3: max=4. From 4: max=4. From 5: max=5. Centroids are 1 and 2. Choose 1. Step 2: Distances from centroid 1: Child subtree 2: distances [2] (leaf 5 at distance 2 via 2-4-5); Child subtree 3: distances [1] (leaf 3). Step 3: Count pairs between: 2+1=3≤3: 1 pair. Step 4: Recursively process subtrees: Subtree rooted at 2 (nodes 2,4,5): centroid=4? Compute: From 2: max=2 (node 5); From 4: max=2 (node 2); From 5: max=3. Centroids 2 and 4. Choose 2. Distances from 2: Child subtree 4: distances [1] (leaf 5). No other child with leaves, so no pairs across. Recursively process subtree rooted at 4: single leaf 5, no pairs. Subtree rooted at 3: single leaf, no pairs. Step 5: Total = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1 pair.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Leaf Pairs Across Subtrees with Distance Constraint
ladders.desc: Given lists of distances from a centroid to leaves in different subtrees, count pairs where sum ≤ D.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because at each centroid, we need to count leaf pairs from different child subtrees whose paths pass through the centroid. The intuition: If leaf A is in subtree i at distance d1 from centroid, and leaf B is in subtree j at distance d2, their path distance is d1 + d2 (since centroid is on the path). We need to count all such pairs where d1 + d2 ≤ D. The algorithm: 1) For each child subtree, collect a list of distances from centroid to all leaves in that subtree. 2) For each pair of child subtrees (i, j where i < j), combine their distance lists and count pairs with sum ≤ D. Efficient counting can be done by sorting each list and using two pointers: sort list A and B, for each a in A, find max b in B such that a + b ≤ D, count all b up to that index. Time: O(k * m log m) where k is number of subtree pairs, m is max list size. Space: O(m). Connection to L0: This is the core counting step in centroid decomposition. Common mistakes: Double-counting pairs within same subtree (should be handled recursively), inefficient O(m²) counting. Real code pattern: def count_pairs_between(dist_list1, dist_list2, D): sort both lists; i=0; j=len(list2)-1; count=0; while i<len(list1): while j>=0 and list1[i]+list2[j]>D: j-=1; count+=(j+1); i+=1; return count.

examples.input: Distances from subtree A: [2,3], from subtree B: [1,4], D=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort A=[2,3], B=[1,4]. Step 2: For a=2: j starts at 1 (value 4), 2+4=6>5 so j-- to 0 (value 1), 2+1=3≤5, count += (0+1)=1. Step 3: For a=3: j=0, 3+1=4≤5, count += (0+1)=1, total=2. Wait, but pairs: (2,1), (2,4) no, (3,1), (3,4) no → only 2 pairs? Let's brute: pairs: (2,1)=3≤5 yes, (2,4)=6 no, (3,1)=4 yes, (3,4)=7 no → 2 pairs. But output says 3. Let's recompute: Actually, we need to count all pairs (a,b) where a in A, b in B. With two-pointer: For a=2: find j such that 2+B[j]≤5. B sorted [1,4]. j=1: 2+4=6>5 → j-- to 0. Now j=0: 2+1=3≤5, so count += number of elements in B from 0 to j inclusive = 1. For a=3: j=0: 3+1=4≤5, count +=1, total=2. So output should be 2. Correcting: Maybe input meant D=6? If D=6: For a=2: j=1: 2+4=6≤6, count+=2; For a=3: j=1: 3+4=7>6 → j-- to 0: 3+1=4≤6, count+=1; total=3. So for D=5, output is 2. I'll adjust: Input D=5, output=2.

examples.input: Distances from subtree A: [1], from subtree B: [2,3], D=4
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort A=[1], B=[2,3]. Step 2: For a=1: j starts at 1 (value 3), 1+3=4≤4, so count += (1+1)=2. Total=2. Pairs: (1,2)=3≤4 yes, (1,3)=4≤4 yes.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Centroid Decomposition for Tree Problems
ladders.desc: Recursively decompose tree by finding centroids to enable divide-and-conquer.
ladders.explanation: Centroid decomposition is a technique to recursively break a tree into smaller subtrees by removing centroids, enabling efficient divide-and-conquer algorithms. The intuition: A centroid is a node whose removal splits the tree into subtrees each of size at most n/2. This guarantees O(log n) depth of recursion, leading to O(n log n) total time for problems where we process O(n) work per level. The algorithm: 1) Compute subtree sizes via DFS. 2) Find a centroid by checking each node: if for all neighbors, subtree size ≤ n/2, it's a centroid. 3) Process the centroid (e.g., count pairs passing through it). 4) Remove centroid and recursively process each resulting subtree. Time: O(n) per level, O(n log n) total. Space: O(n) for recursion and arrays. Connection to L1: This decomposition provides the subtrees for which we count pairs. Common mistakes: Not updating subtree sizes after removal, infinite recursion if centroid condition is wrong. Real code pattern: def decompose(node): centroid = find_centroid(node); process(centroid); for child in centroid.children: if child not removed: decompose(child).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute subtree sizes via DFS from node 1: size(4)=1, size(5)=1, size(2)=1+1+1=3, size(3)=1, size(1)=3+1+1=5. Step 2: Find centroid: Check node 1: neighbor 2 has size 3 > 5/2=2.5, so not centroid. Node 2: neighbors: 1 has size 5-3=2 ≤2.5, 4 has size 1≤2.5, 5 has size 1≤2.5 → all ≤2.5, so centroid is 2. Step 3: Remove centroid 2, subtrees: {4}, {5}, {1,3}. Step 4: Recursively decompose each subtree.

Step 1: Subtree sizes: from 1: size(1)=4, size(2)=3, size(3)=2, size(4)=1. Step 2: Check node 1: neighbor 2 size 3 > 2, not centroid. Node 2: neighbors: 1 size 4-3=1 ≤2, 3 size 2 ≤2 → centroid. Node 3: neighbors: 2 size 4-2=2 ≤2, 4 size 1 ≤2 → centroid. Node 4: neighbor 3 size 3 >2, not centroid. So centroids are 2 and 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Subtree Sizes and Find Centroids in a Tree
ladders.desc: DFS to compute sizes and identify centroid nodes.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves performing a DFS on a tree to compute the size of each node's subtree (number of nodes in the subtree rooted at that node), and then using these sizes to find a centroid. The intuition: Subtree sizes help determine if a node is a centroid by checking if all its neighboring subtrees (when node is removed) have size ≤ n/2. The algorithm: 1) DFS to compute sizes: recursively compute size(node) = 1 + sum(size(child)) for all children. 2) Find centroid: For each node, check if for every neighbor, the size of the subtree on the other side (which is either size(neighbor) if neighbor is child, or n - size(node) if neighbor is parent) is ≤ n/2. If yes, it's a centroid. Time: O(n) for DFS. Space: O(n) for recursion and size array. Connection to L2: This is the first step in centroid decomposition. Common mistakes: Not handling parent-child relationships correctly in size calculation, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def dfs_size(node, parent): size=1; for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor!=parent and not removed[neighbor]: size+=dfs_size(neighbor, node); size_array[node]=size; return size. def find_centroid(node, parent, total_size): for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor!=parent and not removed[neighbor]: if size_array[neighbor] > total_size/2: return find_centroid(neighbor, node, total_size); return node.

examples.trace: Step 1: DFS from node 1: Visit 2 (child of 1): size(2)=1. Visit 3: size(3)=1. Size(1)=1+1+1=3. Step 2: Find centroid: Check node 1: neighbors 2 and 3, both have size 1 ≤ 3/2=1.5, so centroid is 1.

Step 1: DFS from node 1: Visit 2: from 2, visit 3: size(3)=1; size(2)=1+1=2; size(1)=1+2=3. Step 2: Find centroid: Check node 1: neighbor 2 has size 2 > 1.5, not centroid. Node 2: neighbors: 1 has size n-size(2)=3-2=1 ≤1.5, 3 has size 1 ≤1.5, so centroid is 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse tree recursively to visit all nodes and compute properties.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental tree traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition: By recursively visiting each node and its children, we can compute aggregate properties like subtree sizes, distances, or leaf counts. The algorithm: 1) Start at root node. 2) For each neighbor not visited and not removed, recursively call DFS. 3) Process node after visiting children (post-order) to accumulate child information. Time: O(n) where n is number of nodes. Space: O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. Connection to L3: DFS is used to compute subtree sizes. Common mistakes: Forgetting to track visited nodes (leading to cycles in general graphs, but trees are acyclic), stack overflow for deep trees. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent): for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor != parent and not removed[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor, node); process node.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 1. Step 2: Visit neighbor 2 (since not visited). Step 3: At node 2, no unvisited neighbors, return. Step 4: Back to node 1, visit neighbor 3. Step 5: At node 3, no unvisited neighbors, return. Order: 1,2,3.
Step 1: Start at 1. Step 2: Visit 2. Step 3: At 2, visit 3. Step 4: At 3, return to 2. Step 5: At 2, visit 4. Step 6: At 4, return. Order: 1,2,3,4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree Data Structure and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand trees as connected acyclic graphs with nodes and edges.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a fundamental data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges, with no cycles. Key terminology: Node: an element in the tree. Edge: connection between two nodes. Root: the top node (chosen arbitrarily). Parent: the node connected above a given node. Child: a node connected below. Leaf: a node with no children. Subtree: a tree consisting of a node and all its descendants. Depth: distance from root. Height: longest path from node to leaf. The intuition: Trees model hierarchical relationships, and their acyclic nature allows efficient traversal and recursion. Basic operations: Adding nodes, traversing (DFS/BFS). Time: O(n) for traversal. Space: O(n) to store nodes and edges. Connection to L4: Trees are the input for DFS. Common mistakes: Confusing trees with graphs (trees are a special case), not handling empty trees. Real code pattern: Represent tree as adjacency list: graph = {1:[2,3], 2:[1], 3:[1]} for tree 1-2, 1-3.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 1,2,3. Step 2: Edges: 1 connected to 2 and 3. Step 3: Choose root as 1. Step 4: Children of 1: nodes 2 and 3. Step 5: Leaves: nodes 2 and 3 have no children.

Step 1: Nodes: 1,2,3,4. Step 2: Edges: 1-2, 2-3, 2-4. Step 3: Root: 1. Step 4: Children: of 1: 2; of 2: 3,4. Step 5: Leaves: 3 and 4. Step 6: Depth of node 3: from root 1 to 3: path 1-2-3, length 2 edges, so depth 2.
